Pelvis Stress Fractures
Source basis: RadPrimer source package recovered in this audit bundle. Core cross-check was requested and the generated deck claimed Core support, but no auditable Core excerpt/page text was included in this bundle; corrected cards therefore avoid unverifiable Core-only claims.

Key definitions
  • Fatigue fracture: repetitive overuse trauma causes failure of normal bone.
  • Insufficiency fracture: normal activity causes failure of weakened, usually osteoporotic bone; also called fragility fracture.
Imaging spine
  • Radiographs: sclerotic band with or without a lucent fracture line; sacral fractures may be occult because of osteopenia and bowel overlap.
  • CT: shows radiograph-like sclerosis/fracture lines with better sensitivity than radiographs, but remains less sensitive than MRI.
  • MRI: low-signal fracture line on all sequences with surrounding band-like marrow edema; edema is low T1 and high on fluid-sensitive sequences.
  • Nuclear medicine: positive on all phases of a 3-phase bone scan; bone scan may become positive 2-3 weeks before radiographs, may be false negative in the first 72 hours, and uptake can persist for months.
Location framework
  • Insufficiency sites: sacral ala, pubic ramus/body, supraacetabular ilium, and subchondral femoral head.
  • Fatigue sites: medial femoral neck, sacrum in long-distance runners, medial iliac crest, and pubic body.
  • Sacrum: vertical zone 1 or 2 ala fracture may have a transverse component, creating an H-shaped/Honda configuration.
  • Femoral head: subchondral insufficiency fracture line usually parallels the articular surface, unlike osteonecrosis.
Differential pivots
  • Tumor/myeloma/lymphoma: round marrow replacement; stress fracture favors band-like edema and a fracture line, although femoral-neck fatigue edema can look rounded.
  • Osteitis pubis: symmetric pubic symphysis erosions, osteophytes, sclerosis, and subchondral edema without a fracture line.
  • Osteonecrosis: serpentine line curving away from the articular surface rather than a subchondral line paralleling the surface.
  • Sacroiliitis: SI joint widening, erosions, effusion, and edema greater on the iliac side.
  • Osteomyelitis: deep ulcer/abscess context, cortical destruction, marrow edema, and no fracture line.
Mechanism and management
  • Fatigue injury begins with trabecular fracture and marrow edema, then incomplete cortical fracture/callus, and can progress to complete fracture if loading continues.
  • Insufficiency injury reflects normal forces on weakened bone; osteoporosis is most common, with rheumatoid arthritis, radiation osteitis, renal osteodystrophy, and corticosteroid use also listed.
  • Most stress fractures heal with rest, but healing may take 4 weeks to 5 months; nonunion is common in older adults.
  • Femoral neck stress fractures have high risk of progression to complete fracture and may require percutaneous screw fixation.

column1: Older woman with right hip pain M6C2J8Q1Z7LA
column3:
Coronal T2 FS MR in an older woman with right hip pain shows marrow and soft tissue edema around an acute insufficiency fracture of the pubic root .
Axial T2 FS MR in the same patient confirms the presence of marrow and soft tissue edema around an acute insufficiency fracture of the pubic root . Radiographs are often negative in the acute phase.
Nuclear medicine bone scan in the same patient confirms the insufficiency fracture by demonstrating focal uptake at the pubic root . Nuclear medicine bone scan can be performed as an adjunct or alternative to MR in the detection of subtle insufficiency fractures.

Radiographs are often negative in the acute phase
focal uptake at the pubic root
adjunct or alternative to MR

MR demonstrates focal marrow/soft-tissue edema centered at the pubic root, and bone scan confirms focal uptake at the same site. This fits a subtle pelvic insufficiency fracture that may be radiographically occult in the acute phase.

Differential:
Osteitis pubis
Metastatic disease
Osteomyelitis

column3: Coronal T2 FS MR shows a linear fracture line in the subchondral bone of the femoral head with surrounding edema. Because the line is near to and paralleling the articular surfaces, this indicates insufficiency fracture, not osteonecrosis.
column7: MRI hip. Based on the image, name 3 key differentials and give one best discriminator for each.
column8:
Source-supported differentials:
Osteonecrosis of femoral head
Transient osteoporosis of hip
Degenerative joint disease
column9: Dominant pattern differs from other cases because: this drill trains subchondral femoral head line orientation. Osteonecrosis has a serpentine contour that curves away from the articular surface with mottled sclerosis/lucency on radiographs; transient osteoporosis has femoral head edema without a fracture line; degenerative joint disease can cause substantial marrow edema underlying focal cartilage defects rather than a discrete subchondral fracture line parallel to the articular surface.

column11: How does repetitive loading progress from stress reaction to complete fatigue fracture?
column12: Repetitive loading injures normal bone before remodeling can fully repair it. The source describes trabeculae fracturing first, which manifests as marrow edema. With continued stress, the cortex ultimately fractures incompletely and cortical healing produces callus; if activity continues before complete healing, the opposite cortex can fail and the fracture can become complete. Imaging consequence: early MR may show edema, while more advanced injury shows a low-signal fracture line and radiographic/CT sclerosis or cortical thickening.

column11: Why do pelvic insufficiency fractures occur in typical sites despite normal activity?
column12: Insufficiency fracture occurs when normal forces are applied to abnormal, weakened bone. The source states osteoporosis is the most common weakening substrate, with other causes including rheumatoid arthritis, radiation osteitis, renal osteodystrophy, and corticosteroid use. Imaging consequence: weakened pelvic trabecular/cortical bone fails under physiologic loading, producing a low-signal fracture line with surrounding marrow edema; common pelvic sites include sacral ala, pubic ramus/body, supraacetabular ilium, and subchondral femoral head.

column13: What is the pitfall when deciding whether a femoral head subchondral line represents fracture or osteonecrosis, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Calling a subchondral insufficiency fracture osteonecrosis because both can show femoral head marrow edema and low-signal subchondral abnormality.
Safety check: Trace the line orientation.
Key discriminator: Insufficiency fracture line is near and parallel to the articular surface; osteonecrosis has a serpentine contour that curves away from the articular surface and demarcates an osteonecrotic region.
Why the error occurs: Both entities can involve the femoral head and produce subchondral signal abnormality, so the contour/orientation is the deciding clue.

column13: What is the tumor-mimic pitfall in pelvic stress fracture, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Mistaking stress-fracture marrow edema for metastatic disease, myeloma, or lymphoma.
Safety check: Look for a discrete fracture line and the geometry of the marrow abnormality.
Key discriminator: Insufficiency fracture edema is band-like or has straight/rectilinear margins; tumor marrow replacement tends to be rounded and usually lacks a discrete fracture line.
Why the error occurs: Marrow edema can be extensive, and femoral neck fatigue fracture may even have a rounded edema appearance, so CT may be useful when it shows the fracture line more distinctly than MR.

column13: What is the pitfall when evaluating pubic symphysis-region edema, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Calling a pubic root/ramus stress fracture osteitis pubis, or missing a concomitant obturator ramus fracture in a patient with symphyseal degenerative/inflammatory changes.
Safety check: Search specifically for a fracture line and determine whether the process is symmetric at the pubic symphysis.
Key discriminator: Osteitis pubis shows symmetric subchondral marrow edema with erosions, osteophytes, and sclerosis at the pubic symphysis without a fracture line; stress fracture centers on a focal fracture line/edema in the pubic root, pubic ramus, or pubic body.
Why the error occurs: Both can create pubic-region marrow edema and pain, and the source notes they can coexist.

column15: What age/activity profile distinguishes fatigue fractures from pelvic insufficiency fractures?
column16:
Fatigue fracture: young, active adults.
Insufficiency fracture: older population, greater than 55 years.
Exam pivot: Use age/activity to adjust the prior, but still require the fracture-line/edema pattern on imaging.

column15: For pelvis stress fractures, what MRI appearance is expected?
column16:
Fracture line: low signal intensity on all sequences.
Marrow edema: band-like on both sides of fracture; low T1 and high on fluid-sensitive sequences.
Additional findings: enhancement after gadolinium; periosteal reaction and soft-tissue edema are common.
Exam pivot: A straight/band-like edema margin favors fracture over tumor.

column15: For pelvis stress fractures, what radiograph and CT appearance is expected?
column16:
Radiographs: sclerotic band with or without a lucent fracture line.
Sacrum: radiographs may be occult; arcuate line disruption is useful; healing line becomes sclerotic.

Start with radiographs.

column15: What is the sacral insufficiency fracture pattern and key radiographic clue?
column16:
Pattern: vertical sacral ala fracture, zone 1 or 2, with possible transverse component forming an H-shaped/Honda configuration.
Lateral radiograph: may show angulation/buckle at the transverse component, often at S2.
Radiographic clue: disruption of arcuate lines is useful.
Exam pivot: Sacral fractures may be occult due to osteopenia and bowel contents.

column15: What source-supported femoral neck stress-fracture features matter in pelvic stress-fracture imaging?
column16:
Typical setting: athlete or runner with hip pain that increases with activity.
Location/imaging: usually basicervical and medial femoral neck; MRI may show only a small partial low-signal fracture line with surrounding edema.
Pitfall: femoral-neck fatigue fracture can be surrounded by rounded marrow edema and mimic tumor.
Management pivot: high risk of progression to complete fracture, so strict activity management and possible percutaneous screw fixation matter.
Source attribution: RadPrimer Pelvis Stress Fractures source package.

column15: What progression risk makes femoral neck stress fracture high yield in athletes?

Femoral neck stress fractures have high risk of progression to complete fracture if activity/rest is not managed.

Exam pivot: Do not dismiss subtle femoral neck edema/fracture line in an athlete as a benign marrow-edema finding.

column16:
Assess for additional sites of stress/insufficiency fracture.
Always evaluate the sacrum in the setting of obturator stress fracture.
Report evidence of healing response.
Exam pivot: Pelvic stress fractures are often multifocal, so a single visible lesion should not end the search.
column21:
Pelvis Stress Fractures
Source basis: RadPrimer source package recovered in this audit bundle. Core cross-check was requested and the generated deck claimed Core support, but no auditable Core excerpt/page text was included in this bundle; corrected cards therefore avoid unverifiable Core-only claims.

Key definitions
  • Fatigue fracture: repetitive overuse trauma causes failure of normal bone.
  • Insufficiency fracture: normal activity causes failure of weakened, usually osteoporotic bone; also called fragility fracture.
Imaging spine
  • Radiographs: sclerotic band with or without a lucent fracture line; sacral fractures may be occult because of osteopenia and bowel overlap.
  • CT: shows radiograph-like sclerosis/fracture lines with better sensitivity than radiographs, but remains less sensitive than MRI.
  • MRI: low-signal fracture line on all sequences with surrounding band-like marrow edema; edema is low T1 and high on fluid-sensitive sequences.
  • Nuclear medicine: positive on all phases of a 3-phase bone scan; bone scan may become positive 2-3 weeks before radiographs, may be false negative in the first 72 hours, and uptake can persist for months.
Location framework
  • Insufficiency sites: sacral ala, pubic ramus/body, supraacetabular ilium, and subchondral femoral head.
  • Fatigue sites: medial femoral neck, sacrum in long-distance runners, medial iliac crest, and pubic body.
  • Sacrum: vertical zone 1 or 2 ala fracture may have a transverse component, creating an H-shaped/Honda configuration.
  • Femoral head: subchondral insufficiency fracture line usually parallels the articular surface, unlike osteonecrosis.
Differential pivots
  • Tumor/myeloma/lymphoma: round marrow replacement; stress fracture favors band-like edema and a fracture line, although femoral-neck fatigue edema can look rounded.
  • Osteitis pubis: symmetric pubic symphysis erosions, osteophytes, sclerosis, and subchondral edema without a fracture line.
  • Osteonecrosis: serpentine line curving away from the articular surface rather than a subchondral line paralleling the surface.
  • Sacroiliitis: SI joint widening, erosions, effusion, and edema greater on the iliac side.
  • Osteomyelitis: deep ulcer/abscess context, cortical destruction, marrow edema, and no fracture line.
Mechanism and management
  • Fatigue injury begins with trabecular fracture and marrow edema, then incomplete cortical fracture/callus, and can progress to complete fracture if loading continues.
  • Insufficiency injury reflects normal forces on weakened bone; osteoporosis is most common, with rheumatoid arthritis, radiation osteitis, renal osteodystrophy, and corticosteroid use also listed.
  • Most stress fractures heal with rest, but healing may take 4 weeks to 5 months; nonunion is common in older adults.
  • Femoral neck stress fractures have high risk of progression to complete fracture and may require percutaneous screw fixation.

column15: How are complete versus incomplete fractures defined in the source?
column16:
Complete fracture: traverses the entire bone.
Incomplete fracture: visible fracture line that does not traverse the entire bone.
Exam pivot: Incomplete pelvic stress fractures may be subtle but still clinically important, especially at the femoral neck.

column15: What clinical localization clues are source-stated for pelvic stress fractures?
column16:
Pubic ramus stress fracture: groin pain, point tenderness at the inferior pubic ramus, and possible standing sign.
Acetabular stress fracture: anterior hip pain.
Sacral insufficiency fracture: low back pain.
Femoral neck stress fracture: hip pain that increases with activity.
Clinical pattern: runners most often develop femoral neck stress fracture; older osteoporotic patients may present after a fall or with no clear trauma.
Source attribution: RadPrimer Pelvis Stress Fractures source package.

column11: What microscopic/healing features does the source describe for stress fractures?
column12:
The source describes osteoclastic resorption at the fracture site, followed by lamellated bone filling osteoclastic cavities, new periosteal and endosteal bone, and sometimes exuberant periosteal reaction.
Why this matters for imaging: the healing response explains sclerosis/callus on radiographs or CT and why a healing stress fracture can look aggressive or tumor-like if the fracture line is not recognized.
Source attribution: RadPrimer Pelvis Stress Fractures source package. No outside clarification used.

column15: What treatment and prognosis pivots are source-stated for pelvic stress fractures?
column16:
Most stress fractures heal with rest, but healing is slow, ranging from about 4 weeks to 5 months.
Conservative care includes stopping athletic training, rest, NSAIDs, physical therapy, stretching, muscle strengthening, and medical treatment of osteoporosis when relevant.
Older adults have common nonunion risk and may suffer immobility complications such as DVT and decubitus ulcers.
Femoral neck stress fractures are high-risk for completion and may require percutaneous screw fixation.
Sacral insufficiency fractures may be treated with sacroplasty or transiliosacral screw stabilization in selected cases.
Source attribution: RadPrimer Pelvis Stress Fractures source package.

column15: When does the source mention DEXA in pelvic stress-fracture workup?
column16:
DEXA is used to evaluate bone density in patients with insufficiency fractures and in young patients with atypical fractures.
Exam pivot: do not stop at naming the fracture; determine whether weakened bone needs evaluation when the fracture pattern is insufficiency-type or clinically atypical.
